questions.title: SQL Warehouse Type for High Concurrency
questions.question: A team has 50 analysts running simultaneous queries at unpredictable times. Which SQL warehouse type best handles this?

choices.B: Pro warehouse — supports up to 10 clusters for auto-scaling
choices.C: Serverless warehouse — instant start, auto-scales compute
choices.D: Personal cluster — dedicated resources per user

questions.shortAnswer:
Serverless warehouses auto-scale compute instantly based on demand, with no cold start delay
and per-second billing. Ideal for bursty, unpredictable analyst workloads.

questions.title: Serverless vs Pro Warehouse — Start Time
questions.question: A product team needs a SQL warehouse that starts within seconds for morning dashboards. Which type should they choose?

choices.B: Pro — fastest of the dedicated types
choices.C: Serverless — starts in seconds, no warmup needed
choices.D: All warehouse types start in seconds

questions.shortAnswer:
Serverless warehouses are instantly available because compute is managed by Databricks
infrastructure. Classic and Pro warehouses take 2-5 minutes to start.

questions.title: SQL Warehouse Auto-Stop Default
questions.question: What is the default auto-stop idle time for a Databricks SQL warehouse?
questions.shortAnswer:
The default idle auto-stop time is 120 minutes (2 hours). Admins can configure this value
or disable it for always-on warehouses.

questions.title: Connecting External BI Tools
questions.question: A team uses Tableau to connect to Databricks SQL data. Which connection method should they use?

choices.A: JDBC/ODBC driver with warehouse connection details

questions.shortAnswer:
Databricks SQL provides JDBC and ODBC drivers for standard BI tool connectivity. The
connection string includes warehouse hostname, HTTP path, and authentication token.

questions.title: Query History Purpose
questions.question: A data engineer notices a query is running slower than expected in Databricks SQL. Which feature helps diagnose the performance issue?

choices.B: Query History — shows query duration, rows processed, and a visual query plan

questions.shortAnswer:
Databricks SQL Query History provides execution time, rows scanned, the executed SQL, and
a link to the query plan for performance analysis.

questions.title: Pro Warehouse Unique Feature
questions.question: Which capability is available in Pro and Serverless SQL warehouses but NOT in Classic warehouses?

choices.C: Row-level security and column masking via Unity Catalog

questions.shortAnswer:
Row-level security (row access policies) and column masking policies in Unity Catalog
require Pro or Serverless SQL warehouses. Classic warehouses don't support these
fine-grained data access controls.

questions.title: Partner Connect
questions.question: A team wants to connect a third-party ETL tool directly to Databricks without manual credential setup. Which feature should they use?

choices.B: Partner Connect — pre-built integrations with one-click setup

questions.shortAnswer:
Partner Connect provides pre-built, guided integrations with partner tools (dbt, Fivetran,
Tableau, etc.), automating credential setup and warehouse configuration.

questions.title: Multi-Cluster Warehouse
questions.question: A Classic SQL warehouse is overloaded during peak hours, causing queue buildup. What change would allow multiple concurrent query streams?

choices.B: Upgrade to Pro warehouse and enable auto-scaling with Max Clusters > 1

questions.shortAnswer:
Classic warehouses are single-cluster. Pro warehouses support multi-cluster auto-scaling
(1 to 10 clusters), allowing multiple concurrent query streams without queue buildup.

questions.title: Serverless Warehouse Billing
questions.question: Which billing model applies to Serverless SQL warehouses?

choices.A: Per DBU-hour — billed for the full hour whenever active
choices.B: Per DBU-second — billed only for actual compute seconds used

questions.shortAnswer:
Serverless warehouses bill per DBU-second, making them cost-efficient for intermittent
workloads. Classic and Pro warehouses bill per DBU-hour (rounded up).

questions.title: Warehouse Size Selection
questions.question: A data analyst runs complex aggregate queries over 500 GB of Delta Lake data daily. Which warehouse size is most appropriate?

choices.C: Large or X-Large — complex queries over large data benefit from more cores
choices.D: Size doesn't matter — all sizes produce the same result

questions.shortAnswer:
Larger warehouse sizes have more CPU and memory. For complex aggregation over large
datasets, a larger size reduces query time significantly. X-Small is suitable for light,
exploratory queries only.

questions.title: Unity Catalog Three-Level Namespace
questions.question: Which format correctly refers to a table in Unity Catalog?
questions.shortAnswer:
Unity Catalog uses a three-level namespace: `catalog.schema.table`. The two-level
`schema.table` format was used with the legacy Hive metastore.

questions.title: Managed vs External Table DROP
questions.question: A data engineer drops a managed Delta table and then drops an external Delta table. What happens in each case?

choices.A: Both operations delete data files and metadata
choices.C: Managed: deletes data AND metadata. External: deletes metadata only, data files remain
choices.D: External: deletes data AND metadata. Managed: deletes metadata only

questions.title: GRANT SELECT on a Table
questions.question: Which SQL statement correctly grants read access to a table in Unity Catalog?
questions.shortAnswer:
`SELECT` is the correct privilege for read access in Unity Catalog. `READ` and `ACCESS`
are not valid UC privilege names.

questions.title: CREATE TABLE in UC
questions.question: Which statement creates a managed table in the `gold` schema of the `ml_catalog` catalog?

choices.C: `USE CATALOG ml_catalog; CREATE TABLE gold.transactions AS SELECT ...`
choices.D: Both B and C are correct

questions.shortAnswer:
Both a fully-qualified name and `USE CATALOG` + two-level reference create the table in
the correct location.

questions.title: Schema Evolution — mergeSchema
questions.question: An analyst appends data with a new column to an existing Delta table using `df.write.format("delta").mode("append").save(path)`. The new column is not in the table schema. What happens?

choices.A: The new column is automatically added to the table schema
choices.B: An error is raised — new columns are not allowed without explicit schema evolution
choices.C: The new column is silently dropped
choices.D: The write succeeds and the column is stored as a nested JSON string

questions.shortAnswer:
Delta Lake enforces schema-on-write by default. To add new columns, use
`.option("mergeSchema", "true")` or set
`spark.databricks.delta.schema.autoMerge.enabled = true`.

questions.title: overwriteSchema Option
questions.question: What does the `overwriteSchema` option do in a Delta Lake write?

choices.A: Adds new columns to the existing schema
choices.B: Replaces the existing schema entirely with the new DataFrame's schema
choices.C: Validates the DataFrame schema matches the table schema

questions.shortAnswer:
`overwriteSchema=true` (combined with `mode("overwrite")`) replaces the entire table
schema. This is necessary when columns are renamed or dropped, not just added.

questions.title: REVOKE Privilege
questions.question: A data analyst's access to `finance.transactions` must be removed. Which statement is correct?

choices.A: `DELETE GRANT SELECT ON TABLE finance.transactions FROM 'analyst@co.com'`
choices.C: `DROP GRANT SELECT ON TABLE finance.transactions FROM 'analyst@co.com'`

questions.shortAnswer: `REVOKE` is the correct SQL command to remove privileges in Unity Catalog.

questions.title: Metastore vs Catalog
questions.question: In Unity Catalog, what is the relationship between a metastore and a catalog?

choices.A: They are the same thing — different names for the same object
choices.B: A metastore is the top-level account-level container; a catalog is an object within the metastore
choices.D: A metastore is created per workspace; a catalog is account-level

questions.shortAnswer:
A Unity Catalog metastore is created once per region at the account level. Multiple
catalogs exist within a metastore. Workspaces are attached to the metastore.

questions.title: Delta Lake TBLPROPERTIES
questions.question: Which statement sets a custom property on a Delta table?
questions.shortAnswer:
`ALTER TABLE ... SET TBLPROPERTIES` is the correct syntax. Properties are key-value
string pairs.

questions.title: Data Lineage in UC Explorer
questions.question: A compliance team wants to see which dashboards read from the `gold.revenue_metrics` table. Which UC feature provides this?

choices.C: Unity Catalog data lineage graph in the Catalog Explorer UI

questions.shortAnswer:
Unity Catalog automatically captures column-level and table-level lineage. The Catalog
Explorer UI shows upstream (sources) and downstream (consumers) for any table.

questions.title: External Location
questions.question: An analyst creates an external table pointing to `s3://company-data/raw/sales/`. The underlying S3 files are deleted by a pipeline mistake. What happens when the table is queried?

choices.B: An error is raised — the files referenced by the external table no longer exist
choices.C: Delta Lake automatically restores the files from the transaction log

questions.shortAnswer:
External tables point to external storage. If the files are deleted, queries fail with a
file-not-found error. Delta can only restore files if they were managed and within VACUUM
retention.

questions.title: USE CATALOG Statement
questions.question: A data analyst runs `USE CATALOG analytics; USE SCHEMA reporting;`. What does `SELECT * FROM revenue` query?
questions.shortAnswer:
`USE CATALOG` sets the default catalog and `USE SCHEMA` sets the default schema.
Unqualified table references use these defaults: `analytics.reporting.revenue`.

questions.title: INNER JOIN vs LEFT JOIN
questions.question:
A query needs all employees and their department names, including employees without a department.
Which JOIN type should be used?

choices.A: INNER JOIN — returns only matching rows
choices.B: LEFT JOIN — returns all rows from the left table, NULL for unmatched right rows
choices.D: SEMI JOIN — checks for existence only

questions.shortAnswer:
LEFT JOIN preserves all rows from the left table (employees). Employees without a department
get NULL in the department name column. INNER JOIN would exclude employees with no department.

questions.title: Window Function — ROW_NUMBER
questions.question:
A query must return one row per customer with the highest purchase amount. Which window function
assigns a unique rank per customer?

choices.A: `RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY amount DESC)`
choices.B: `DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY amount DESC)`
choices.C: `ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY amount DESC)`
choices.D: `NTILE(1) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY amount DESC)`

questions.shortAnswer:
`ROW_NUMBER()` assigns 1 to the top row per partition with no ties (always unique). Filter
`WHERE rn = 1` to get one row per customer. `RANK()` and `DENSE_RANK()` can produce duplicates
when there are ties.

questions.title: RANK vs DENSE_RANK
questions.question:
Sales figures are: Alice=100, Bob=100, Carol=90. Using `RANK()` and `DENSE_RANK()`, what are
Carol's values?
questions.shortAnswer:
With two tied rows at rank 1, `RANK()` skips to 3 for the next row (gaps exist). `DENSE_RANK()`
assigns 2 (no gaps). Alice and Bob both get rank 1 in both functions.

questions.title: LAG Function
questions.question:
A query calculates the change in monthly sales vs the previous month. Which function retrieves the
previous month's value?
questions.shortAnswer:
`LAG(column, offset)` returns the value from `offset` rows before the current row within the
window. `LEAD` looks forward; `LAG` looks backward. `PREV` and `OFFSET` are not valid Databricks
SQL functions.

questions.title: QUALIFY Clause
questions.question:
An analyst wants to return only the top-ranked product per category (based on revenue), using
window functions. Which clause filters on the window function result?

choices.C: `QUALIFY ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY category ORDER BY revenue DESC) = 1`

questions.shortAnswer:
`QUALIFY` filters on window function expressions directly. `WHERE` executes before window
functions; `HAVING` filters on aggregates after GROUP BY. `QUALIFY` is the correct clause for
filtering window results without wrapping in a subquery.

questions.title: Running Total
questions.question:
An analyst needs a running total of sales, ordered by date, within each region. Which expression
computes this?

choices.B: `SUM(sales) OVER (PARTITION BY region ORDER BY date)`
choices.D: `CUMSUM(sales) OVER (PARTITION BY region ORDER BY date)`

questions.shortAnswer:
`SUM(sales) OVER (PARTITION BY region ORDER BY date)` computes a cumulative sum within each
region, increasing with each row sorted by date. Without `ORDER BY`, it returns the total sum
for the entire partition. `CUMSUM` is not a valid Databricks SQL function.

questions.title: CTE vs Subquery
questions.question: Which statement about CTEs (Common Table Expressions) is TRUE?

choices.A: CTEs are always faster than equivalent subqueries
choices.B: CTEs improve readability and can be referenced multiple times in the same query
choices.C: CTEs cannot be used in SELECT statements
choices.D: CTEs are materialized by default in Databricks SQL

questions.shortAnswer:
CTEs improve readability by naming intermediate result sets. They can be referenced multiple
times in the same query without repeating logic. Performance is typically equivalent to
subqueries — both are logical constructs, not necessarily materialized.

questions.title: EXPLODE for Arrays
questions.question:
A table has a `tags` column of type `ARRAY<STRING>`. A query needs one row per tag. Which function
produces this?
questions.shortAnswer:
`EXPLODE(array_column)` creates one row per array element. `FLATTEN` combines nested arrays into
a flat array without exploding rows. `UNNEST` is PostgreSQL syntax, not used in Databricks SQL.
`SPLIT` converts a string to an array but does not expand rows.

questions.title: COLLECT_LIST Aggregation
questions.question: Which aggregate function combines multiple row values into an array?
questions.shortAnswer:
Both `COLLECT_LIST` (Spark SQL) and `ARRAY_AGG` (SQL standard alias) aggregate multiple values
into an array column. `GROUP_CONCAT` and `STRING_AGG` produce a delimited string, not an array.
`CONCAT_WS` concatenates strings with a separator but is not an aggregate.

questions.title: ANTI JOIN
questions.question: A query needs all customers who have NOT made a purchase. Which JOIN type filters for non-matches?

choices.C: Both A and B are correct approaches

questions.shortAnswer:
Both approaches work. `LEFT ANTI JOIN` is the explicit syntax for returning only non-matching
left rows. `LEFT JOIN ... WHERE right_key IS NULL` is the equivalent filter approach. The
explicit `ANTI JOIN` syntax is often clearer in intent.

questions.title: PIVOT Syntax
questions.question:
An analyst wants to pivot monthly sales into columns (Jan, Feb, Mar). Which SQL feature
accomplishes this?

choices.A: `CASE WHEN month = 'Jan' THEN sales END` in SELECT

questions.shortAnswer:
`PIVOT` reshapes rows into columns. Both conditional aggregation (A) and PIVOT (B) produce the
same result, but `PIVOT` syntax is more concise. Full syntax:
`FROM table PIVOT (agg(col) FOR pivot_col IN (values))`. `TRANSPOSE` is not a valid Databricks
SQL function.

questions.title: HAVING vs WHERE
questions.question:
An analyst writes a query with `WHERE COUNT(*) > 5` and gets an error. What is the correct
approach?
questions.shortAnswer:
`WHERE` filters individual rows before aggregation; aggregate functions like `COUNT()` cannot
appear in a `WHERE` clause. `HAVING` filters after GROUP BY aggregation and supports aggregate
expressions. `QUALIFY` filters window function results, not aggregate results.

questions.title: Date Function
questions.question: Which function returns the number of days between `order_date` and today?
questions.shortAnswer:
`DATEDIFF(end_date, start_date)` is the Databricks SQL function for date difference in days.
Note the argument order: end date first, then start date. `DATE_DIFF` (with underscore) is not
the standard Databricks function name. `DAYS_BETWEEN` does not exist in Databricks SQL.

questions.title: String Function — SUBSTRING
questions.question: Which expression extracts characters 2 through 5 from the `product_code` column?
questions.shortAnswer:
`SUBSTR(string, start_pos, length)` where start is 1-indexed. Starting at position 2 with
length 4 extracts characters 2, 3, 4, 5. Option C has the wrong length parameter — starting at
position 2 with length 5 would extract characters 2 through 6. `MID` is MySQL syntax not used
in Databricks SQL.

questions.title: EXPLAIN Statement
questions.question:
A data analyst wants to understand why a SQL query is performing slowly. Which statement provides
the query execution plan?
questions.shortAnswer:
`EXPLAIN SELECT ...` shows the logical and physical query plan including joins, scans, and
filters. In Databricks SQL, the query history UI also shows a visual plan for completed queries.
`DESCRIBE` describes table schemas, not query plans. `PROFILE` and `ANALYZE SELECT` are not
valid Databricks SQL statements.

questions.title: NULL Handling in Aggregations
questions.question: A column `revenue` has some NULL values. What does `SUM(revenue)` return?

choices.A: NULL — if any value is NULL, the aggregate is NULL
choices.C: An error — cannot aggregate columns with NULLs
choices.D: 0 — NULLs are treated as 0 in aggregations

questions.shortAnswer:
SQL aggregate functions (SUM, AVG, COUNT, MIN, MAX) ignore NULL values by default. Only
`COUNT(*)` counts NULLs; `COUNT(column)` ignores them. `SUM` returns NULL only if ALL values
in the column are NULL. NULLs are never silently treated as 0.

questions.title: SEMI JOIN
questions.question: A query returns all products that have at least one review. Which JOIN type is most appropriate?

choices.C: LEFT SEMI JOIN — returns left rows where match exists, no duplication

questions.shortAnswer:
`LEFT SEMI JOIN` returns rows from the left table (products) where a match exists in the right
table (reviews), without producing duplicate product rows. INNER JOIN can produce multiple rows
per product when a product has multiple reviews, requiring a DISTINCT or GROUP BY to deduplicate.

questions.title: Subquery in WHERE
questions.question: Which query returns employees earning more than the average salary?

choices.B: `SELECT * FROM employees WHERE salary > (SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees)`

questions.shortAnswer:
Aggregate functions cannot appear in `WHERE` clauses of the outer query. A scalar subquery in
the `WHERE` clause computes the average separately and passes it as a value for comparison.
`MEAN` is not a standard SQL aggregate function in Databricks SQL — use `AVG`.

questions.title: Dashboard Widget Types
questions.question: Which widget type displays a single aggregated number prominently (e.g., total revenue)?
questions.shortAnswer:
Counter widgets display a single prominent metric value, ideal for KPIs and summary stats on
dashboards. Table widgets display multi-column rows, line charts show trends over time, and
text widgets display static markdown content.

questions.title: Lakeview vs Legacy Dashboards
questions.question: What is the primary advantage of Lakeview Dashboards over legacy dashboards in Databricks SQL?

choices.B: Lakeview has a modern drag-and-drop canvas UI with improved layout flexibility

questions.shortAnswer:
Lakeview Dashboards (introduced in 2024) provide a modernized canvas-based editor with flexible
layout, improved sharing, and better visualization options compared to the legacy dashboard
editor. Performance depends on the underlying queries and warehouse, not the dashboard type.
Row-level security is a Unity Catalog feature independent of dashboard type.

questions.title: Dashboard Refresh Schedule
questions.question:
A business stakeholder needs a dashboard to refresh automatically every morning at 8 AM. How
should this be configured?

choices.A: The dashboard refreshes automatically when opened — no configuration needed
choices.B: Set a schedule on the dashboard using a SQL warehouse for execution
choices.C: Create a Databricks Job that triggers a notebook to refresh the dashboard
choices.D: Dashboards cannot be scheduled — only individual queries can

questions.shortAnswer:
Dashboards can have a scheduled refresh in Databricks SQL. Configure the schedule (cron or
interval) and assign a SQL warehouse to run the underlying queries at the specified time.
The warehouse must be running or set to auto-start for scheduled refresh to succeed.

questions.title: SQL Alert Condition
questions.question:
An analyst creates a SQL alert on a query that returns the count of failed transactions. The alert
should fire when the count exceeds 100. How should the alert condition be configured?
questions.shortAnswer:
SQL alerts support `>`, `<`, `=`, `>=`, `<=`, and `IS NULL` as threshold conditions. Setting
`> 100` causes the alert to fire whenever the query result exceeds 100. Option D is not a valid
single alert condition — alerts support one condition at a time.

questions.title: Alert Notification Destination
questions.question:
A SQL alert needs to send a message to a Slack channel when triggered. Which configuration enables
this?

choices.A: Add a Slack webhook URL as a notification destination in the alert settings
choices.B: Write a Python script that polls the alert status
choices.C: Configure a Databricks Job to check the alert query result
choices.D: Slack integration is not supported — use email only

questions.shortAnswer:
Databricks SQL alerts support multiple notification destinations: email, Slack (via webhook),
PagerDuty, and custom webhooks. Add the Slack incoming webhook URL in the alert's notification
settings. No custom scripting is required for this native integration.

questions.title: Dashboard Sharing
questions.question:
A manager wants to share a Databricks SQL dashboard with external stakeholders who don't have
Databricks accounts. Which sharing option allows this?

choices.B: Publish the dashboard and share the public URL (if enabled by admin)
choices.C: Export to PDF and email it
choices.D: Use Delta Sharing to expose the dashboard data

questions.shortAnswer:
Databricks SQL dashboards can be published to create a shareable URL that allows viewing without
a Databricks login (requires admin to enable anonymous access or external sharing). Granting
workspace access requires creating user accounts. PDF export is a manual workaround and does not
stay up to date. Delta Sharing exposes data, not dashboards.

questions.title: Chart Visualization — Bar vs Line
questions.question:
Sales data needs to show trend over time for 12 months. Which visualization type is most
appropriate?

choices.A: Bar chart — best for time series trends
choices.B: Line chart — shows trends and changes over time clearly

questions.shortAnswer:
Line charts are ideal for time series data, clearly showing trends, peaks, and patterns over
time. Bar charts work better for comparing discrete categories. Scatter plots show the
relationship between two variables. Pie charts show part-to-whole proportions.

questions.title: Table Widget Use Case
questions.question: Which scenario is best suited for a table widget in a Databricks SQL dashboard?

choices.A: Showing total revenue as a KPI number
choices.C: Showing the top 20 products with their revenue, units sold, and rank
choices.D: Showing the proportion of sales by region

questions.shortAnswer:
Table widgets display tabular data with multiple columns and rows, ideal for detailed lists and
comparisons. KPIs use counter widgets (A), trends over time use line charts (B), and proportions
use pie or donut charts (D).

questions.title: Alert Schedule
questions.question:
A SQL alert monitors inventory below a reorder threshold. The business needs notification within
1 hour of the condition occurring. What schedule frequency is appropriate?
questions.shortAnswer:
Set the alert refresh to 1-hour intervals so that an inventory drop below threshold is detected
and notified within one hour of occurring. Databricks SQL alerts are not real-time streaming;
they run on a polling schedule. 24-hour refresh would miss the SLA. Manual refresh provides no
automation.

questions.title: Dashboard Parameter Syntax
questions.question:
A dashboard has a date range filter that passes the selected date to multiple queries. Which syntax
is used in SQL queries to accept dashboard parameters?
questions.shortAnswer:
Databricks SQL uses `{{parameter_name}}` (double curly braces, also called mustache syntax) for
query parameters and dashboard filters. The parameter name inside the braces must match the
parameter widget name configured on the dashboard.

questions.title: Query Parameter Type
questions.question:
A dashboard has a dropdown for selecting a region that filters all queries. What is the parameter
widget type?

choices.B: Dropdown list — allows selection from predefined values

questions.shortAnswer:
Dropdown list parameters let users choose from a predefined set of values (e.g., regions). The
selected value is substituted into `{{region}}` in the SQL query. Text boxes allow any input,
date pickers are for calendar dates, and number inputs are for numeric ranges.

questions.title: Multi-Value Parameter
questions.question:
An analyst needs a filter that allows selecting multiple product categories at once. Which SQL
syntax handles multiple values from a multi-select dropdown?
questions.shortAnswer:
When a parameter supports multiple values, they are substituted as a comma-separated list.
`IN ({{categories}})` correctly handles single or multiple selected values. Using `= '{{...}}'`
only matches a single value. `LIKE` and `CONTAINS` do not handle multi-value substitution.

questions.title: Scheduled Query Use Case
questions.question:
A data team runs a heavy aggregation query daily to populate a summary table for analyst
dashboards. Which approach reduces dashboard load time?

choices.A: Have each dashboard query run the aggregation when opened
choices.B: Schedule the aggregation query to run nightly and write results to a Delta table; dashboard
choices.C: Use a larger SQL warehouse so aggregation runs faster on-demand
choices.D: Enable query caching on the dashboard

questions.shortAnswer:
Pre-computing aggregates with a scheduled query and storing results in a summary Delta table
dramatically reduces dashboard query time. On-demand aggregation of raw data slows every user's
experience. A larger warehouse (C) reduces run time but still runs the expensive query per
session. Query caching (D) helps for repeated identical queries but does not help when underlying
data changes.

questions.title: Row-Level Security
questions.question:
A sales dashboard must show each sales rep only their own region's data. Which feature implements
this transparently without requiring separate dashboards?

choices.A: Dashboard-level filter set by each user
choices.B: A WHERE clause hardcoded for each user in the dashboard query
choices.C: Row-level security (row access policy) on the underlying Delta table via Unity Catalog

questions.shortAnswer:
Row-level security in Unity Catalog applies dynamic filters based on the current user's identity,
transparently enforcing data access without requiring separate objects or manual filter
configuration. This works at the table level, so the policy applies to all queries regardless
of how the table is accessed. Available with Pro or Serverless SQL warehouses.

questions.title: Dashboard Embedding
questions.question:
A company wants to embed a Databricks SQL dashboard in their internal web portal for non-Databricks
users. Which capability enables this?

choices.B: REST API polling for dashboard screenshots
choices.C: Dashboard embedding using the published URL or iFrame embedding (if enabled by admin)
choices.D: Export to HTML and host separately

questions.shortAnswer:
Published Databricks dashboards can be embedded in external applications via iFrame using the
public URL. Admin must enable external sharing or anonymous access in workspace settings. Delta
Sharing (A) exposes data, not dashboards. REST API polling (B) is not a supported embedding
method. HTML export (D) would produce a static snapshot, not a live dashboard.

questions.title: Alert Refresh vs Query Refresh
questions.question: What is the difference between refreshing a dashboard query and refreshing a SQL alert?

choices.A: They are the same — both use the same schedule configuration
choices.B: Dashboard refresh updates visualization data; SQL alert refresh evaluates the alert condition
choices.C: Dashboard refresh is automatic; SQL alerts never auto-refresh
choices.D: SQL alerts only work on scheduled queries, not dashboard queries

questions.shortAnswer:
Dashboard refresh updates the data shown in visualizations. SQL alert refresh runs the alert
query and checks whether the trigger condition is met, sending notifications if true. They are
independently configured objects in Databricks SQL and can have different schedules.

questions.title: Query Result Caching
questions.question:
Databricks SQL query result caching can improve performance. Under which condition does the cache
NOT apply?

choices.A: The query runs within 24 hours of the last execution
choices.B: The underlying Delta table has been modified since the last query execution
choices.C: The same query text is used
choices.D: The same SQL warehouse is used

questions.shortAnswer:
Databricks SQL caches query results but invalidates the cache when the underlying data changes.
If new data was written to the Delta table, the cache is stale and the query runs fresh against
the current data. Cache hits require identical query text, the same warehouse, and no data
changes in the source table.
